content.text: When JavaScript checks whether two values are equal, it does very different things depending on whether those values are primitives or objects. This distinction — value equality versus reference equality — is one of the most important concepts for React developers to understand, because React itself depends on reference equality to know when to re-render.
Primitive types (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol) are compared
. Two primitives are equal if their values are identical:
When you assign a primitive to a variable and then assign that variable to another, you get a
. Changing one doesn't affect the other:
Objects (including arrays and functions) are stored differently. When you create an object, JavaScript allocates memory and stores a
(a pointer to that memory location). Variables hold the reference, not the object itself.
Two objects are only equal if they point to the
— even if their contents look identical:

Think of a reference like a street address on a piece of paper. Two people can each write "123 Main St" on a separate piece of paper — those are two different papers (different references), even though the text says the same address. When you assign
, it's like giving someone a copy of your address card — both cards now point to the same house.

React uses reference equality in several places to decide whether something has changed and whether a re-render is needed. This is the most important practical consequence of value vs. reference equality for React developers.
When you call a state setter, React compares the new value to the old value using
). For objects and arrays, this is a reference check:

's dependency array is compared element by element using reference equality. An object or array created inline in a component re-renders as a new reference every render, which can cause effects to re-fire unexpectedly:
prevents re-rendering a child component if its props haven't changed. The comparison is shallow: each prop is checked with
. If a parent passes an inline object or array as a prop, it's a new reference every render, defeating memoization:
Functions defined inside a component are created fresh on every render, giving them a new reference each time. Passing such a function to a memoized child or including it in a
React does NOT do deep equality. It uses shallow

comparisons everywhere for performance. This is why creating new object references when updating state is both required (to trigger re-renders) and important to do carefully (to avoid unnecessary re-renders in memoized components).
To check if two objects have the same content without a library, you have to compare properties manually or use

, even if their contents are identical.
Mutating state objects doesn't trigger re-renders
because the reference stays the same. Always return a new object or array.
Inline objects and functions in JSX
create new references on every render, defeating memoization. Use
JavaScript has no built-in deep equality. Use
for simple cases or a library like lodash for nested structures.
